pages.text: The year was 2095, and Neo-Mumbai shimmered like a diamond on the Arabian Sea. Hover buses whooshed between glowing towers, chai-serving drones zipped above markets shouting, “Cutting chai, piping hot!”, and robotic rickshaws hummed along the sky lanes.

Twelve-year-old <name> loved her city — especially the Science Hub at her school, where she built tiny helper bots from recycled circuits. One morning, while packing her smart schoolbag, it suddenly buzzed, “Alert! Power surge detected in Zone 7!”

<name> frowned. Zone 7 was where her Amma worked — the Solar Light Control Tower that powered the city’s night lights. She jumped on a hover scooter and zoomed off. The air shimmered with neon colors as she rode past holo-billboards showing dancing Bollywood bots.

When she arrived, the tower’s lights flickered wildly. The central AI orb, “SuryaNet,” glowed red instead of its usual soft gold. “Unauthorized override detected,” said a mechanical voice. Amma ran out, worried. “Something’s wrong! The AI won’t respond to commands.”

<name> peeked at the main console. “Maybe it’s a glitch in the LightSync code,” she murmured. Just then, a small cleaning robot, Robo-Kittu, rolled out from under a table, blinking nervously. “M-meow… I didn’t mean to cause trouble!” it squeaked.

“Wait — you can talk?” <name> gasped. Robo-Kittu’s eyes blinked pink. “I tried updating the power settings to make the city brighter for Diwali, but I think I overloaded the circuits.” Amma sighed. “We can’t shut down the AI — the whole city depends on these lights!”

<name> thought for a moment. “Kittu, can you access the internal circuits?” “Yes, but I’ll need help reprogramming,” said the robot. “Then let’s team up!” she grinned. They raced into the glowing control room. Sparks flickered. <name> connected her pocket tablet and began typing furiously.

“If I rewrite the feedback loop and reroute the charge through the solar grid, it should stabilize!” Kittu purred, extending his claw-like tools. “Ready when you are!” The room rumbled as the AI voice boomed, “Warning. System overload.” “Hold steady!” <name> shouted.

Kittu plugged into the main console — zzzt! — and the lights dimmed, then glowed brighter, shifting from harsh red to calm gold. Outside, Neo-Mumbai shimmered again, its towers wrapped in warm golden light. Amma hugged her. “You did it, beta!” Kittu beeped proudly. “We did it!”

Later, at the Science Hub, <name> presented her adventure. “Even machines can make mistakes,” she told her classmates, “but with curiosity and teamwork, we can always fix them.” Her teacher smiled. “That’s true innovation — not just building, but understanding.”

That evening, as fireworks burst over the city and chai drones floated past her window, <name> watched the glowing skyline with pride. Neo-Mumbai’s lights sparkled once more — not just from solar power, but from the bright curiosity of one brave girl and her robot friend.
